name: Muros de Nalón, Quinta de Selgas, Cudillero, Monasterio San Salvador de Cornellana — circular from Soto del Barco
name_original: Muros de Nalón, Quinta de Selgas, Cudillero, Monasterio San Salvador de Cornellana — circular desde Soto del Barco
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Soto Del Barco traverse a diverse landscape at the mouth of the Nalón River, along the Cantabrian coast in Asturias, Spain. The region features a coastal "rasa" of plains and lower elevations, interspersed with wetlands, dune systems, and marshes, particularly around the Nalón River estuary. While generally accessible, some routes include more pronounced hills in areas like the Sierra de Mafalla and Fontebona. Best touring cycling routes around Soto Del Barco The most popular touring cycling route is Muros de Nalón, Quinta de Selgas y Playa de Aguilar — circular desde Espíritu Santo, a 10.0 miles (16.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 8 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the coast and passes by the historic Quinta de Selgas. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Muros de Nalón, Quinta de Selgas, Cudillero, Monasterio San Salvador de Cornellana — circular from Soto del Barco, a difficult 34.5 miles (55.6 km) path. This route connects several towns and historical sites, providing a varied touring experience. Local touring cyclists also love the Bridge over the Nalón River – Muros de Nalón loop from San Esteban de Pravia, a 38.8 miles (62.5 km) trail leading through the Nalón River estuary and coastal areas, often completed in about 4 hours 34 minutes. Touring cycling around Soto Del Barco is defined by coastal paths, river estuaries, and rolling hills, offering routes for various abilities. The region's network includes options for both challenging road cycling and more accessible paths. The routes in Soto Del Barco are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 129 reviews. More than 1900 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Soto Del Barco's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Soto Del Barco?
A: There are nearly 80 touring cycling routes in Soto Del Barco, offering a wide range of experiences for cyclists. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 129 reviews.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Soto Del Barco?
A: Soto Del Barco offers a diverse landscape for touring cyclists. You'll find routes traversing the coastal 'rasa' with plains and lower elevations, as well as paths through wetlands, dune systems, and marshes, especially around the Nalón River estuary. While generally accessible, some routes include more pronounced hills in areas like the Sierra de Mafalla and Fontebona, providing options for various abilities.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Soto Del Barco has several easier routes. There are 7 easy touring cycling routes available. While specific family-friendly routes aren't detailed, the region's generally gentle relief along the coast and river estuary makes it accessible for a wide range of cycling abilities. Look for routes with lower elevation gains for a more relaxed experience.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see along the cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer glimpses of the region's rich history and natural beauty. You can cycle past the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/353344" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Selgas Palace and the Jesús Nazareno Church‑Pantheon&lt;/a&gt;, often called the 'Versailles of Asturias,' or the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/353340" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de San Martín&lt;/a&gt; overlooking the Nalón River's mouth. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2005855" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Concha de Artedo Viaduct&lt;/a&gt; is another impressive sight along some coastal paths.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many routes in Soto Del Barco are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. For example, the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029989151/muros-de-nalon-quinta-de-selgas-y-playa-de-aguilar-circular-desde-espiritu-santo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muros de Nalón, Quinta de Selgas y Playa de Aguilar — circular desde Espíritu Santo&lt;/a&gt; offers coastal views and passes by the historic Quinta de Selgas. Another option is the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029986901/muros-de-nalon-quinta-de-selgas-cudillero-monasterio-san-salvador-de-cornellana-circular-from-soto-del-barco" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muros de Nalón, Quinta de Selgas, Cudillero, Monasterio San Salvador de Cornellana — circular from Soto del Barco&lt;/a&gt;, which connects several towns and historical sites.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Soto Del Barco?
A: Soto Del Barco, being on the Cantabrian coast, experiences marine mists, which are a characteristic weather phenomenon. Generally, spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures for cycling. Summer can be warm but also provides refreshing coastal breezes. Winters are milder than inland areas but can be damp. Always check local weather forecasts before heading out.

Q: Can I find routes that are part of the Camino de Santiago?
A: Yes, Soto Del Barco is a significant draw for touring cyclists as it forms part of the historic Camino de Santiago, specifically the Coastal Path through Asturias (GR 204). This section is renowned for its beauty, offering a unique blend of pilgrimage and scenic cycling.

Q: Are there any long-distance or more challenging touring routes?
A: Absolutely. For those seeking a challenge, there are 46 difficult touring cycling routes in the area. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40302004" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bridge over the Nalón River – Cabo Peñas Lighthouse loop from San Esteban de Pravia&lt;/a&gt;, a demanding 86.4 km route. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40320951" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bridge over the Nalón River – Muros de Nalón loop from San Esteban de Pravia&lt;/a&gt;, covering 62.5 km through the estuary and coastal areas.

Q: What kind of natural features will I encounter while cycling?
A: The region's natural beauty is defined by its unique geographical position at the mouth of the Nalón River and along the Cantabrian Sea. Cyclists will encounter dynamic environments with wetlands, dune systems, and marshes, particularly around the protected landscape of Los Quebrantos beach. The interplay of river, estuary, and sea provides stunning vistas and diverse ecosystems.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the Nalón River estuary?
A: Yes, the Nalón River estuary is a central feature of many touring cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20498434" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muros de Nalón – Bridge over the Nalón River loop from Soto del Barco/Sotu'l Barcu&lt;/a&gt; is a 35.7 km route that leads directly through the estuary and coastal areas, offering beautiful riverine landscapes.

Q: What do other cyclists say about touring in Soto Del Barco?
A: The touring cycling routes in Soto Del Barco are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars. Over 1900 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore the area, often praising the varied terrain that combines coastal paths, river estuaries, and rolling hills, catering to different abilities and offering a rich touring experience.
tips: The Nalón river becomes at its mouth between San Esteban de Pravia and San Juan de la Arena in the Pravia estuary. The Nalón is 153 kilometers long and rises in the port of Tarna, in the mountains of Caso.

A small coastal municipality of just over 1,800 inhabitants. It is worth seeing the stately town of Muros (of Roman and medieval origin), the walls of the old Palace of Valdecarzana and the Palace of the Marqués de Muros, and its old mansions and beautiful buildings.

The Quinta de Selgas is a 19th century palace built on the initiative of the brothers Ezequiel and Fortunato de Selgas Albuerne. Those who wanted to have a palace in the Asturian lands in the Italian way and endow it with important artistic pieces, furniture and household items from renowned houses. Its collection includes pieces by Goya and El Greco. It also has an outstanding collection of tapestries, porcelain and glassware. The entire property is surrounded by a wall and has its own church and a beautifully landscaped garden.

Cudillero is the epitome of Asturian fishing villages nestled on a hillside with access to the sea. Its state of conservation is magnificent and its steep streets are full of colorful houses. Although its main economic activity is still fishing, in summer tourism floods its streets. If you want to eat some good grilled sardines, you are in the perfect place.

According to legend, little Cristina, daughter of King Bermudo II de Léon, got lost in the deep forest. It would have been certain death if a bear hadn't nursed her to the rescue and protected her from wild animals. Long after that, in 1024, when the monastery was founded, she had the scene immortalized in stone: the bear and the child can be seen above the archway in front of the courtyard of the pilgrim hostel.
